column1: NASA Artemis II Heads to the Moon
Mission Is the First Crewed Lunar Journey Since 1972
The crew of three Americans and one Canadian launched from Kennedy Space Center in Florida and embarked on a 10-day journey around the moon and back.
“We have a beautiful moonrise and we’re headed right at it,” Reid Wiseman, the Artemis II mission commander, said from the spacecraft as it ascended atop the rocket.
The final 10 minutes, which NASA calls “terminal count,” is controlled by a computer. A launch controller can halt the countdown if something doesn’t look right.
The countdown has resumed. Counting down for 6:35 and 12 seconds p.m. Eastern time.
Artemis II is a go for launch. The launch team is working to set a definite launch time.
The countdown clock is holding at 10 minutes as NASA works through issues. That means the launch will not occur at 6:24 p.m., but there is plenty of time remaining in the two-hour window.
The issue with the battery in the launch abort system has been resolved. The engineers concluded that the temperature reading in the battery is faulty, not a problem with the battery.
Another problem has cropped up. This time it is battery on the launch abort system on Orion, which ejects the capsule and astronauts away from the rocket if there is a problem.
The outer hatch of Orion has been closed, sealing the crew inside the space capsule ahead of launch, and the close-out crew is getting ready to leave.
I don’t envy the forecasters making the “go or no-go” call for the launch today. A single rogue rain cloud can ruin everything.
Rain showers have been recently popping up within 20 miles of the launchpad, but satellite imagery shows the breeze pushing most of that moisture inland. Still, some risk remains of a no-go call.
The issue with the rocket’s flight termination system that had the potential to postpone the launch is resolved, said Derrol Nail, a NASA commentator. Everything else seems to be proceeding smoothly.
Derrol Nail, a NASA commentator, said engineers are trying to address an issue with the Space Launch System rocket’s flight termination system. The system must work before a rocket launches.
Technicians are checking to make sure that there are no leaks after closing the inner hatch of Orion. Tight seals are critical for maintaining air inside the capsule.
Thousands assembled hours before launch at the Cocoa Beach Pier, about 20 miles south of the Kennedy Space Center. A rainstorm left watchers soaked, but gave way to dry skies and sunshine.
The four astronauts are now in their seats in the Orion capsule, which the astronauts named Integrity. The close-out crew is now assisting them before they close up the capsule.
The crew has boarded the van that will take them to the launchpad. NASA commentators said the astronauts will be watching “Top Gun” and “Point Break” during the ride.
All of the liquid oxygen and liquid hydrogen tanks are now full. That allows the close-out crews to go to the pad for final preparations there.
NASA’s launch director for the mission gave the “go” to fill the propellant tanks of the Space Launch System rocket with liquid hydrogen and liquid oxygen. It is a key step in preparing for liftoff.
